As an application to Fourier analysis, recall the inner product space \(H\) and the orthonormal set \(S\) in Example 9 of Section 6.1. Define a trigonometric polynomial of degree \(n\) to be a function \(g \in \mathrm{H}\) of the form

\[ g(t)=\sum_{j=-n}^{n} a_{j} f_{j}(t)=\sum_{j=-n}^{n} a_{j} c^{i j t} . \]

where \(a_{n}\) or \(a_{-n}\) is nonzero. Let \(f \in \mathrm{H}\). We show that the best approximation to \(f\) by a trigonometric polynomial of degree less than or equal to \(n\) is the trigonometric polynomial whose coefficients are the Fourier coefficients of \(f\) relative to the orthonormal set \(S\). For this result, let \(\mathrm{W}=\operatorname{span}\left(\left\{f_{j}:|j| \leq n ight\} ight)\), and let T be the orthogonal projection of H on W. The corollary to Theorem 6.6 (p. 350) tells us that the best approximation to \(f\) by a function in \(W\) is

\[ \mathrm{T}(f)=\sum_{j=-n}^{n}\left\langle f . f_{j} ight angle f_{j} \]

An algebraic characterization of orthogonal projections follows in the next theorem.

Theorem 6.24. Let \(V\) be an inner product space, and let \(T\) be a linear operator on V . Then T is an orthogonal projection if and only if T has an adjoint \(\mathrm{T}^{*}\) and \(\mathrm{T}^{2}=\mathrm{T}=\mathrm{T}^{*}\).

Proof. Suppose that \(T\) is an orthogonal projection. Since \(T^{2}=T\) because \(T\) is a projection, we need only show that \(T^{*}\) exists and \(T=T^{*}\). Now \(\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{R}(\mathrm{T}) \oplus \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})\) and \(\mathrm{R}(\mathrm{T})^{\perp}=\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})\). Let \(x, y \in \mathrm{~V}\). Then we can write \(x=x_{1}+x_{2}\) and \(y=y_{1}+y_{2}\), where \(x_{1}, y_{1} \in \mathrm{R}(\mathrm{T})\) and \(x_{2}, y_{2} \in \mathrm{~N}(\mathrm{~T})\). Hence

\[ \langle x \cdot \mathrm{~T}(y) angle=\left\langle x_{1}+x_{2}, y_{1} ight angle=\left\langle x_{1}, y_{1} ight angle+\left\langle x_{2}, y_{1} ight angle=\left\langle x_{1}, y_{1} ight angle \]

and

\[ \langle\mathrm{T}(x), y angle=\left\langle x_{1}, y_{1}+y_{2} ight angle=\left\langle x_{1}, y_{1} ight angle+\left\langle x_{1}, y_{2} ight angle=\left\langle x_{1}, y_{1} ight angle \]

So \(\langle x, \mathrm{~T}(y) angle=\langle\mathrm{T}(x), y angle\) for all \(x, y \in \mathrm{~V}\); thus \(\mathrm{T}^{*}\) exists and \(\mathrm{T}=\mathrm{T}^{*}\). Now suppose that \(T^{2}=T=T^{*}\). Then \(T\) is a projection by Exercise 17 of Section 2.3, and hence we must, show that \(R(T)=N(T)^{\perp}\) and \(R(T)^{\perp}=N(T)\). Let \(x \in \mathrm{R}(\mathrm{T})\) and \(y \in \mathrm{~N}(\mathrm{~T})\). Then \(x=\mathrm{T}(x)=\mathrm{T}^{*}(x)\), and so

\[ \langle x \cdot y angle=\left\langle\mathrm{T}^{*}(r) \cdot y ight angle=\langle x, \mathrm{~T}(y) angle=\langle x \cdot 0 angle=0 . \]

Therefore \(x \in \mathrm{~N}(\mathrm{~T})^{\perp}\), from which it follows that \(\mathrm{R}(\mathrm{T}) \subseteq \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})^{\perp}\). Let \(y \in \mathrm{~N}(\mathrm{~T})^{\perp}\). We must show that \(y \in \mathrm{R}(\mathrm{T})\), that is, \(\mathrm{T}(y)=y\). Now

\[ egin{aligned} \|y-\mathrm{T}(y)\|^{2} & =\langle y-\mathrm{T}(y), y-\mathrm{T}(y) angle \ & =\langle y \cdot y-\mathrm{T}(y) angle-\langle\mathrm{T}(y) \cdot y-\mathrm{T}(y) angle . \end{aligned} \]

Since \(y-\mathrm{T}(y) \in \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})\), the first term must equal zero. But also

\[ \left.\langle\mathrm{T}(y), y-\mathrm{T}(y) angle=\left\langle y, \mathrm{~T}^{*}(y-\mathrm{T}(y)) ight angle=\langle y \cdot \mathrm{~T}(y-\mathrm{T}(y)) angle=\langle y \cdot 0 angle ight angle=0 . \]

Thus \(y-\mathrm{T}(y)=0\); that is, \(y=\mathrm{T}(y) \in \mathrm{R}(\mathrm{T})\). Hence \(\mathrm{R}(\mathrm{T})=\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T}) \downarrow\). Using the preceding results, we have \(R(T)^{\perp}=N(T)^{\perp \perp} \supseteq N(T)\) by Exercise \(13(\mathrm{~b})\) of Section 6.2. Now suppose that \(x \in \mathrm{R}(\mathrm{T})^{\perp}\). For any \(y \in \mathrm{~V}\), we have \(\langle\mathrm{T}(x), y angle=\left\langle x, \mathrm{~T}^{*}(y) ight angle=\langle x . \mathrm{T}(y) angle=0\). So \(\mathrm{T}(x)=0\), and thus \(x \in \mathrm{~N}(\mathrm{~T})\). Hence \(R(T)^{\perp}=N(T)\).

Let \(V\) be a finite-dimensional inner product space, \(W\) be a subspace of \(V\), and \(T\) be the orthogonal projection of V on W . We may choose an orthonormal basis \( eta=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n} ight\}\) for V such that \(\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{k} ight\}\) is a basis for W . Then \([T]_{ eta}\) is a diagonal matrix with ones as the first \(k\) diagonal entries and zeros elsewhere. In fact, \([T]_{ eta}\) has the form

\[ \left( egin{array}{ll} I_{k} & O_{1} \ O_{2} & O_{3} \end{array} ight) \]

If U is any projection on W , we may choose a basis \(\gamma\) for V such that \([\mathrm{U}]_{\gamma}\) has the form above; however \(\gamma\) is not necessarily orthonormal.

We are now ready for the principal theorem of this section. Theorem 6.25 (The Spectral Theorem). Suppose that T is a linear operator on a finite-dimensional inner product space V over \(F\) with the distinct eigenvalues \(\lambda_{1}, \lambda_{2}, \ldots, \lambda_{k}\). Assume that \(T\) is normal if \(F=C\) and that T is self-adjoint if \(F=R\). For each \(i(1 \leq i \leq k)\), let \(\mathrm{W}_{i}\) be the eigenspace of T corresponding to the eigenvalue \(\lambda_{i}\), and let \(\mathrm{T}_{i}\) be the orthogonal projection of V on \(\mathrm{W}_{i}\). Then the following statements are true. (a) \(\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{W}_{1} \oplus \mathrm{~W}_{2} \oplus \cdots \oplus \mathrm{~W}_{k}\). (b) If \(W_{i}^{\prime}\) denotes the direct sum of the subspaces \(W_{j}\) for \(j eq i\), then \(W_{i}^{\perp}=W_{i}^{\prime}\). (c) \(\mathrm{T}_{i} \mathrm{~T}_{j}=\delta_{i j} \mathrm{~T}_{i}\) for \(1 \leq i, j \leq k\). (d) \( oldsymbol{I}=\mathrm{T}_{1}+\mathrm{T}_{2}+\cdots+\mathrm{T}_{k}\). (e) \(\mathrm{T}=\lambda_{1} \mathrm{~T}_{1}+\lambda_{2} \mathrm{~T}_{2}+\cdots+\lambda_{k} \mathrm{~T}_{k}\).

Proof. (a) By Theorems 6.16 (p. 372) and 6.17 (p. 374), T is diagonalizable; so

\[ \mathrm{V}=\mathrm{W}_{1} \oplus \mathrm{~W}_{2} \oplus \cdots \oplus \mathrm{~W}_{k} \]

by Theorem 5.11 (p. 278). (b) If \(x \in \mathrm{~W}_{i}\) and \(y \in \mathrm{~W}_{j}\) for some \(i eq j\), then \(\langle x, y angle=0\) by Theorem \(6.15(\mathrm{~d})\) (p. 371). It follows easily from this result that \(\mathrm{W}_{i}^{\prime} \subseteq \mathrm{W}_{i}^{\perp}\). From (a), we have

\[ \operatorname{dim}\left(\mathrm{W}_{i}^{\prime} ight)=\sum_{j eq i} \operatorname{dim}\left(\mathrm{~W}_{j} ight)=\operatorname{dim}(\mathrm{V})-\operatorname{dim}\left(\mathrm{W}_{i} ight) \]

On the other hand, we have \(\operatorname{dim}\left(W_{i}^{1} ight)=\operatorname{dim}(V)-\operatorname{dim}\left(W_{i} ight)\) by Theorem 6.7(c) (p. 352). Hence \(W_{i}^{\prime}=W_{i}^{\perp}\), proving (b). (c) The proof of (c) is left as an exercise. (d) Since \(\mathrm{T}_{i}\) is the orthogonal projection of V on \(\mathrm{W}_{i}\), it follows from (b) that \(\mathrm{N}\left(\mathrm{T}_{i} ight)=\mathrm{R}\left(\mathrm{T}_{i} ight)^{\perp}=\mathrm{W}_{i}^{\perp}=\mathrm{W}_{i}^{\prime}\). Hence, for \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\), we have \(x=\) \(x_{1}+x_{2}+\cdots+x_{k}\), where \(\mathrm{T}_{i}(x)=x_{i} \in \mathrm{~W}_{i}\), proving (d). (e) For \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\), write \(x=x_{1}+x_{2}+\cdots+x_{k}\), where \(x_{i} \in \mathrm{~W}_{i}\). Then

\[ egin{aligned} \mathrm{T}(x) & =\mathrm{T}\left(x_{1} ight)+\mathrm{T}\left(x_{2} ight)+\cdots+\mathrm{T}\left(x_{k} ight) \ & =\lambda_{1} x_{1}+\lambda_{2} x_{2}+\cdots+\lambda_{k} x_{k} \ & =\lambda_{1} \mathrm{~T}_{1}(x)+\lambda_{2} \mathrm{~T}_{2}(x)+\cdots+\lambda_{k} \mathrm{~T}_{k}(x) \ & =\left(\lambda_{1} \mathrm{~T}_{1}+\lambda_{2} \mathrm{~T}_{2}+\cdots+\lambda_{k} \mathrm{~T}_{k} ight)(x) . \end{aligned} \]

The set \(\left\{\lambda_{1}, \lambda_{2}, \ldots, \lambda_{k} ight\}\) of eigenvalues of \(T\) is called the spectrum of \(T\), the sum \(I=T_{1}+T_{2}+\cdots+T_{k}\) in (d) is called the resolution of the identity operator induced by \(T\), and the sum \(T=\lambda_{1} T_{1}+\lambda_{2} T_{2}+\cdots+\lambda_{k} T_{k}\) in (e) is called the spectral decomposition of T . The spectral decomposition of T is unique up to the order of its eigenvalues.

With the preceding notation, let \( eta\) be the union of orthonormal bases of the \(W_{i}\) 's and let \(m_{i}=\operatorname{dim}\left(W_{i} ight)\). (Thus \(m_{i}\) is the multiplicity of \(\lambda_{i}\).) Then \([T]_{ eta}\) has the form

\[ \left( egin{array}{cccc} \lambda_{1} I_{m_{1}} & O & \cdots & O \ O & \lambda_{2} I_{m_{2}} & \cdots & O \ \vdots & \vdots & & \vdots \ O & O & \cdots & \lambda_{k} I_{m_{k}} \end{array} ight) \]

that is, \([\mathrm{T}]_{ eta}\) is a diagonal matrix in which the diagonal entries are the eigenvalues \(\lambda_{i}\) of \(T\), and each \(\lambda_{i}\) is repeated \(m_{i}\) times. If \(\lambda_{1} T_{1}+\lambda_{2} T_{2}+\cdots+\lambda_{k} T_{k}\) is the spectral decomposition of \(T\), then it follows (from Exercise 7) that \(g(\mathrm{~T})=g\left(\lambda_{1} ight) \mathrm{T}_{1}+g\left(\lambda_{2} ight) \mathrm{T}_{2}+\cdots+g\left(\lambda_{k} ight) \mathrm{T}_{k}\) for any polynomial \(g\). This fact is used below.

We now list several interesting corollaries of the spectral theorem; many more results are found in the exercises. For what follows, we assume that T is a linear operator on a finite-dimensional inner product space V over \(F\).

Corollary 1. If \(F=C_{2}\), then T is normal if and only if \(\mathrm{T}^{*}=g(\mathrm{~T})\) for some polynomial \(g\).

Proof. Suppose first that \(T\) is normal. Let \(T=\lambda_{1} T_{1}+\lambda_{2} T_{2}+\cdots+\lambda_{k} T_{k}\) be the spectral decomposition of \(T\). Taking the adjoint of both sides of the preceding equation, we have \(T^{*}= ar{\lambda}_{1} T_{1}+ ar{\lambda}_{2} T_{2}+\cdots+ ar{\lambda}_{k} T_{k}\) since each \(T_{i}\) is self-adjoint. Using the Lagrange interpolation formula (see page 52 ), we may choose a polynomial \(g\) such that \(g\left(\lambda_{i} ight)= ar{\lambda}_{i}\) for \(1 \leq i \leq k\). Then

\[ g(\mathrm{~T})=g\left(\lambda_{1} ight) \mathrm{T}_{1}+g\left(\lambda_{2} ight) \mathrm{T}_{2}+\cdots+g\left(\lambda_{k} ight) \mathrm{T}_{k}= ar{\lambda}_{1} \mathrm{~T}_{1}+ ar{\lambda}_{2} \mathrm{~T}_{2}+\cdots+ ar{\lambda}_{k} \mathrm{~T}_{k}=\mathrm{T}^{*} \]

Conversely, if \(\mathrm{T}^{*}=g(\mathrm{~T})\) for some polynomial \(g\), then T commutes with \(\mathrm{T}^{*}\) since T commutes with every polynomial in T . So T is normal.

Corollary 2. If \(F=C\), then T is unitary if and only if T is normal and \(|\lambda|=1\) for every eigenvalue \(\lambda\) of \(T\).

Proof. If \(T\) is unitary, then \(T\) is normal and every eigenvalue of \(T\) has absolute value 1 by Corollary 2 to Theorem 6.18 (p. 382).

Let \(T=\lambda_{1} T_{1}+\lambda_{2} T_{2}+\cdots+\lambda_{k} T_{k}\) be the spectral decomposition of \(T\). If \(|\lambda|=1\) for every eigenvalue \(\lambda\) of \(T\), then by (c) of the spectral theorem,

\[ egin{aligned} \mathrm{TT}^{*} & =\left(\lambda_{1} \mathrm{~T}_{1}+\lambda_{2} \mathrm{~T}_{2}+\cdots+\lambda_{k} \mathrm{~T}_{k} ight)\left( ar{\lambda}_{1} \mathrm{~T}_{1}+ ar{\lambda}_{2} \mathrm{~T}_{2}+\cdots+ ar{\lambda}_{k} \mathrm{~T}_{k} ight) \ & =\left|\lambda_{1} ight|^{2} \mathrm{~T}_{1}+\left|\lambda_{2} ight|^{2} \mathrm{~T}_{2}+\cdots+\left|\lambda_{k} ight|^{2} \mathrm{~T}_{k} \ & =\mathrm{T}_{1}+\mathrm{T}_{2}+\cdots+\mathrm{T}_{k} \ & =\mathrm{I} . \end{aligned} \]

Hence \(T\) is unitary. Corollary 3. If \(F=C\) and \(T\) is normal, then \(T\) is self-adjoint if and only if every eigenvalue of \(T\) is real.

Proof. Let T \(=\lambda_{1} \mathrm{~T}_{1}+\lambda_{2} \mathrm{~T}_{2}+\cdots+\lambda_{k} \mathrm{~T}_{k}\) be the spectral decomposition of T. Suppose that every eigenvalue of \(T\) is real. Then

\[ \mathrm{T}^{*}= ar{\lambda}_{1} \mathrm{~T}_{1}+ ar{\lambda}_{2} \mathrm{~T}_{2}+\cdots+ ar{\lambda}_{k} \mathrm{~T}_{k}=\lambda_{1} \mathrm{~T}_{1}+\lambda_{2} \mathrm{~T}_{2}+\cdots+\lambda_{k} \mathrm{~T}_{k}=\mathrm{T} \]

The converse has been proved in the lemma to Theorem 6.17 (p. 374).

Corollary 4. Let T be as in the spectral theorem with spectral decomposition \(T=\lambda_{1} T_{1}+\lambda_{2} T_{2}+\cdots+\lambda_{k} T_{k}\). Then each \(T_{j}\) is a polynomial in T.

Proof. Choose a polynomial \(g_{j}(1 \leq j \leq k)\) such that \(g_{j}\left(\lambda_{i} ight)=\delta_{i j}\). Then

\[ egin{aligned} g_{j}(\mathrm{~T}) & =g_{j}\left(\lambda_{1} ight) \mathrm{T}_{1}+g_{j}\left(\lambda_{2} ight) \mathrm{T}_{2}+\cdots+g_{j}\left(\lambda_{k} ight) \mathrm{T}_{k} \ & =\delta_{1 j} \mathrm{~T}_{1}+\delta_{2 j} \mathrm{~T}_{2}+\cdots+\delta_{k j} \mathrm{~T}_{k}=\mathrm{T}_{j} . \end{aligned} \]

docs.title: EXERCISES
docs.text:
1. Label the following statements as true or false. Assume that the underlying inner product spaces are finite-dimensional. (a) All projections are self-adjoint. (b) An orthogonal projection is uniquely determined by its range. (c) Every self-adjoint operator is a linear combination of orthogonal projections. (d) If T is a projection on W , then \(\mathrm{T}(x)\) is the vector in W that is closest to \(x\). (e) Every orthogonal projection is a unitary operator.
  2. Let \(V=R^{2}, W=\operatorname{span}(\{(1,2)\})\), and \(\beta\) be the standard ordered basis for \(V\). Compute \([T]_{\beta}\), where \(T\) is the orthogonal projection of \(V\) on \(W\). Do the same for \(V=R^{3}\) and \(W=\operatorname{span}(\{(1,0,1)\})\).
  3. For each of the matrices \(A\) in Exercise 2 of Section 6.5: (1) Verify that \(L_{A}\) possesses a spectral decomposition. (2) For each eigenvalue of \(L_{A}\), explicitly define the orthogonal projection on the corresponding eigenspace. (3) Verify your results using the spectral theorem.
  4. Let \(W\) be a finite-dimensional subspace of an inner product space \(V\). Show that if \(T\) is the orthogonal projection of \(V\) on \(W\), then \(I-T\) is the orthogonal projection of V on \(\mathrm{W}^{\perp}\).
  5. Let T be a linear operator on a finite-dimensional inner product space V . (a) If T is an orthogonal projection, prove that \(\|\mathrm{T}(x)\| \leq\|x\|\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\). Give an example of a projection for which this inequality does not hold. What can be concluded about a projection for which the inequality is actually an equality for all \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\) ? (b) Suppose that T is a projection such that \(\|\mathrm{T}(x)\| \leq\|x\|\) for \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\). Prove that \(T\) is an orthogonal projection.
  6. Let T be a normal operator on a finite-dimensional inner product space. Prove that if T is a projection, then T is also an orthogonal projection.
  7. Let \(T\) be a normal operator on a finite-dimensional complex inner product space \(V\). Use the spectral decomposition \(\lambda_{1} T_{1}+\lambda_{2} T_{2}+\cdots+\lambda_{k} T_{k}\) of \(T\) to prove the following results. (a) If \(g\) is a polynomial, then
\[ g(\mathrm{~T})=\sum_{i=1}^{k} g\left(\lambda_{i}\right) \mathrm{T}_{i} \]

(b) If \(\mathrm{T}^{n}=\mathrm{T}_{0}\) for some \(n\), then \(\mathrm{T}=\mathrm{T}_{0}\). (c) Let U be a linear operator on V . Then U commutes with T if and only if \(U\) commutes with each \(T_{i}\). (d) There exists a normal operator \(U\) on \(V\) such that \(U^{2}=T\). (e) T is invertible if and only if \(\lambda_{i} \neq 0\) for \(1 \leq i \leq k\). (f) T is a projection if and only if every eigenvalue of T is 1 or 0 . (g) \(\mathrm{T}=-\mathrm{T}^{*}\) if and only if every \(\lambda_{i}\) is an imaginary number. 8. Use Corollary 1 of the spectral theorem to show that if \(T\) is a normal operator on a complex finite-dimensional inner product space and \(U\) is a linear operator that commutes with \(T\), then \(U\) commutes with \(T^{*}\). 9. Referring to Exercise 20 of Section 6.5, prove the following facts about a partial isometry U. (a) \(\mathrm{U}^{*} \mathrm{U}\) is an orthogonal projection on W . (b) \(U U^{*} U=U\). 10. Simultaneous diagonalization. Let \(U\) and \(T\) be normal operators on a finite-dimensional complex inner product space \(V\) such that \(T U=U T\). Prove that there exists an orthonormal basis for \(V\) consisting of vectors that are eigenvectors of both \(T\) and \(U\). Hint: Use the hint of Exercise 14 of Section 6.4 along with Exercise 8. 11. Prove (c) of the spectral theorem.

docs.title: 6.7* THE SINGULAR VALUE DECOMPOSITION AND THE PSEUDOINVERSE
docs.text:
In Section 6.4, we characterized normal operators on complex spaces and selfadjoint operators on real spaces in terms of orthonormal bases of eigenvectors and their corresponding eigenvalues (Theorems 6.16, p. 372, and 6.17, p. 374). In this section, we establish a comparable theorem whose scope is the entire class of linear transformations on both complex and real finite-dimensional inner product spaces-the singular value theorem for linear transformations (Theorem 6.26). There are similarities and differences among these theorems. All rely on the use of orthonormal bases and numerical invariants. However, because of its general scope, the singular value theorem is concerned with two (usually distinct) inner product spaces and with two (usually distinct) orthonormal bases. If the two spaces and the two bases are identical, then the transformation would, in fact, be a normal or self-adjoint operator. Another difference is that the numerical invariants in the singular value theorem, the singular values, are nonnegative, in contrast to their counterparts, the eigenvalues, for which there is no such restriction. This property is necessary to guarantee the uniqueness of singular values.

The singular value theorem encompasses both real and complex spaces. For brevity, in this section we use the terms unitary operator and unitary matrix to include orthogonal operators and orthogonal matrices in the context of real spaces. Thus any operator T for which \(\langle\mathrm{T}(x), \mathrm{T}(y)\rangle=\langle x, y\rangle\), or any matrix \(A\) for which \(\langle A x, A y\rangle=\langle x, y\rangle\), for all \(x\) and \(y\) is called unitary for the purposes of this section.

In Exercise 15 of Section 6.3, the definition of the adjoint of an operator is extended to any linear transformation \(\mathrm{T}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow \mathrm{W}\), where V and W are finite-dimensional inner product spaces. By this exercise, the adjoint \(\mathrm{T}^{*}\) of T is a linear transformation from W to V and \(\left[\mathrm{T}^{*}\right]_{\gamma}^{\beta}=\left([\mathrm{T}]_{\beta}^{\gamma}\right)^{*}\), where \(\beta\) and \(\gamma\) are orthonormal bases for \(V\) and \(W\), respectively. Furthermore, the linear operator \(T^{*} T\) on \(V\) is positive semidefinite and \(\operatorname{rank}\left(T^{*} T\right)=\operatorname{rank}(T)\) by Exercise 18 of Section 6.4.

With these facts in mind, we begin with the principal result. Theorem 6.26 (Singular Value Theorem for Linear Transformations). Let V and W be finite-dimensional inner product spaces, and let \(\mathrm{T}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow \mathrm{W}\) be a linear transformation of rank \(r\). Then there exist orthonormal bases \(\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}\) for V and \(\left\{u_{1}, u_{2}, \ldots, u_{m}\right\}\) for W and positive scalars \(\sigma_{1} \geq \sigma_{2} \geq \cdots \geq \sigma_{r}\) such that

\[ T\left(v_{i}\right)= \begin{cases}\sigma_{i} u_{i} & \text { if } 1 \leq i \leq r \tag{4}\\ 0 & \text { if } i>r\end{cases} \]

Conversely, suppose that the preceding conditions are satisfied. Then for \(1 \leq i \leq n, v_{i}\) is an eigenvector of \(\mathrm{T}^{*} \mathrm{~T}\) with corresponding eigenvalue \(\sigma_{i}^{2}\) if \(1 \leq i \leq r\) and 0 if \(i>r\). Therefore the scalars \(\sigma_{1}, \sigma_{2}, \ldots, \sigma_{r}\) are uniquely determined by \(T\).

Proof. We first establish the existence of the bases and scalars. By Exercises 18 of Section 6.4 and \(15(\mathrm{~d})\) of Section \(6.3, T^{*} T\) is a positive semidefinite linear operator of rank \(r\) on V ; hence there is an orthonormal basis \(\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}\) for V consisting of eigenvectors of \(\mathrm{T}^{*} \mathrm{~T}\) with corresponding eigenvalues \(\lambda_{i}\), where \(\lambda_{1} \geq \lambda_{2} \geq \cdots \geq \lambda_{r}>0\), and \(\lambda_{i}=0\) for \(i>r\). For \(1 \leq i \leq r\), define \(\sigma_{i}=\sqrt{\lambda_{i}}\) and \(u_{i}=\frac{1}{\sigma_{i}} \mathrm{~T}\left(v_{i}\right)\). We show that \(\left\{u_{1}, u_{2}, \ldots, u_{r}\right\}\) is an orthonormal subset of W . Suppose \(1 \leq i, j \leq r\). Then.

\[ \begin{aligned} \left\langle u_{i}, u_{j}\right\rangle & =\left\langle\frac{1}{\sigma_{i}} \mathrm{~T}\left(v_{i}\right), \frac{1}{\sigma_{j}} \mathrm{~T}\left(v_{j}\right)\right\rangle \\ & =\frac{1}{\sigma_{i} \sigma_{j}}\left\langle\mathrm{~T}^{*} \mathrm{~T}\left(v_{i}\right), v_{j}\right\rangle \\ & =\frac{1}{\sigma_{i} \sigma_{j}}\left\langle\lambda_{i} v_{i}, v_{j}\right\rangle \\ & =\frac{\sigma_{i}^{2}}{\sigma_{i} \sigma_{j}}\left\langle v_{i}, v_{j}\right\rangle \\ & =\delta_{i j} \end{aligned} \]

and hence \(\left\{u_{1}, u_{2}, \ldots, u_{r}\right\}\) is orthonormal. By Theorem 6.7(a) (p. 352), this set extends to an orthonormal basis \(\left\{u_{1}, u_{2}, \ldots, u_{r}, \ldots, u_{m}\right\}\) for \(W\). Clearly \(\mathrm{T}\left(v_{i}\right)=\sigma_{i} u_{i}\) if \(1 \leq i \leq r\). If \(i>r\), then \(\mathrm{T}^{*} \mathrm{~T}\left(v_{i}\right)=0\), and so \(\mathrm{T}\left(v_{i}\right)=0\) by Exercise 15(d) of Section 6.3.

To establish uniqueness, suppose that \(\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\},\left\{u_{1}, u_{2}, \ldots, u_{m}\right\}\), and \(\sigma_{1} \geq \sigma_{2} \geq \cdots \geq \sigma_{r}>0\) satisfy the properties stated in the first part of the theorem. Then for \(1 \leq i \leq m\) and \(1 \leq j \leq n\),

\[ \begin{aligned} \left\langle\mathrm{T}^{*}\left(u_{i}\right), v_{j}\right\rangle & =\left\langle u_{i}, \mathrm{~T}\left(v_{j}\right)\right\rangle \\ & = \begin{cases}\sigma_{i} & \text { if } i=j \leq r \\ 0 & \text { otherwise }\end{cases} \end{aligned} \]

and hence for any \(1 \leq i \leq m\),

\[ \mathrm{T}^{*}\left(u_{i}\right)=\sum_{j=1}^{n}\left\langle\mathrm{~T}^{*}\left(u_{i}\right), v_{j}\right\rangle v_{j}= \begin{cases}\sigma_{i} v_{i} & \text { if } i=j \leq r \tag{5}\\ 0 & \text { otherwise }\end{cases} \]

So for \(i \leq r\),

\[ \mathrm{T}^{*} \mathrm{~T}\left(v_{i}\right)=\mathrm{T}^{*}\left(\sigma_{i} u_{i}\right)=\sigma_{i} \mathrm{~T}^{*}\left(u_{i}\right)=\sigma_{i}^{2} v_{i} \]

and \(\mathrm{T}^{*} \mathrm{~T}\left(v_{i}\right)=\mathrm{T}^{*}(0)=0\) for \(i>r\). Therefore each \(v_{i}\) is an eigenvector of \(\mathrm{T}^{*} \mathrm{~T}\) with corresponding eigenvalue \(\sigma_{i}^{2}\) if \(i \leq r\) and 0 if \(i>r\).

Definition. The unique scalars \(\sigma_{1}, \sigma_{2}, \ldots, \sigma_{r}\) in Theorem 6.26 are called the singular values of T . If \(r\) is less than both \(m\) and \(n\), then the term singular value is extended to include \(\sigma_{r+1}=\cdots=\sigma_{k}=0\), where \(k\) is the minimum of \(m\) and \(n\).

Although the singular values of a linear transformation \(T\) are uniquely determined by T , the orthonormal bases given in the statement of Theorem 6.26 are not uniquely determined because there is more than one orthonormal basis of eigenvectors of \(\mathrm{T}^{*} \mathrm{~T}\).

In view of (5), the singular values of a linear transformation \(T: V \rightarrow W\) and its adjoint T* are identical. Furthermore, the orthonormal bases for V and W given in Theorem 6.26 are simply reversed for \(\mathrm{T}^{*}\).

docs.title: Example 1
docs.text:
Let \(\mathrm{P}_{2}(R)\) and \(\mathrm{P}_{1}(R)\) be the polynomial spaces with inner products defined by

\[ \langle f(x), g(x)\rangle=\int_{-1}^{1} f(t) g(t) d t . \]

Let \(\mathrm{T}: \mathrm{P}_{2}(R) \rightarrow \mathrm{P}_{1}(R)\) be the linear transformation defined by \(\mathrm{T}(f(x))=\) \(f^{\prime}(x)\). Find orthonormal bases \(\beta=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, v_{3}\right\}\) for \(\mathrm{P}_{2}(R)\) and \(\gamma=\left\{u_{1}, u_{2}\right\}\) for \(\mathrm{P}_{1}(R)\) such that \(\mathrm{T}\left(v_{i}\right)=\sigma_{i} u_{i}\) for \(i=1,2\) and \(\mathrm{T}\left(v_{3}\right)=0\), where \(\sigma_{1} \geq \sigma_{2}>0\) are the nonzero singular values of \(T\).

To facilitate the computations, we translate this problem into the corresponding problem for a matrix representation of \(T\). Caution is advised here because not any matrix representation will do. Since the adjoint is defined in terms of inner products, we must use a matrix representation constructed from orthonormal bases for \(\mathrm{P}_{2}(R)\) and \(\mathrm{P}_{1}(R)\) to guarantee that the adjoint of the matrix representation of \(T\) is the same as the matrix representation of the adjoint of T. (See Exercise 15 of Section 6.3.) For this purpose, we use the results of Exercise 21(a) of Section 6.2 to obtain orthonormal bases

\[ \alpha=\left\{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} x, \sqrt{\frac{5}{8}}\left(3 x^{2}-1\right)\right\} \quad \text { and } \quad \alpha^{\prime}=\left\{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} x\right\} \]

for \(\mathrm{P}_{2}(R)\) and \(\mathrm{P}_{1}(R)\), respectively. Let

\[ A=[T]_{\alpha}^{\alpha^{\prime}}=\left(\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & \sqrt{3} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \sqrt{15} \end{array}\right) \]

Then

\[ A^{*} A=\left(\begin{array}{cc} 0 & 0 \\ \sqrt{3} & 0 \\ 0 & \sqrt{15} \end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & \sqrt{3} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \sqrt{15} \end{array}\right)=\left(\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 15 \end{array}\right) \]

which has eigenvalues (listed in descending order of size) \(\lambda_{1}=15, \lambda_{2}=3\), and \(\lambda_{3}=0\). These cigenvalues correspond, respectively, to the orthonormal cigenvectors \(e_{3}=(0,0,1), e_{2}=(0,1,0)\), and \(e_{1}=(1,0,0)\) in \(\mathrm{R}^{3}\). Translating everything into the context of \(\mathrm{T} . \mathrm{P}_{2}(R)\). and \(\mathrm{P}_{1}(R)\), let

\[ v_{1}=\sqrt{\frac{5}{8}}\left(3 x^{2}-1\right), \quad v_{2}=\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} x, \quad \text { and } \quad v_{3}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} . \]

Then \(\beta=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, v_{3}\right\}\) is an orthonormal basis for \(\mathrm{P}_{2}(R)\) consisting of eigenvectors of T* \(T\) with corresponding eigenvalues \(\lambda_{1}, \lambda_{2}\), and \(\lambda_{3}\). Now set \(\sigma_{1}=\sqrt{\lambda_{1}}=\sqrt{15}\) and \(\sigma_{2}=\sqrt{\lambda_{2}}=\sqrt{3}\), the nonzero singular values of T , and take

\[ u_{1}=\frac{1}{\sigma_{1}} T\left(v_{1}\right)=\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} x \quad \text { and } \quad u_{2}=\frac{1}{\sigma_{2}} T\left(v_{2}\right)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \]

to obtain the required basis \(\gamma=\left\{u_{1}, u_{2}\right\}\) for \(\mathrm{P}_{1}(R)\).

We can use singular values to describe how a figure is distorted by a lincar transformation. This is illustrated in the next example.

docs.title: Example 2
docs.text:
Let T be an invertible linear operator on \(\mathrm{R}^{2}\) and \(S=\left\{x \in \mathrm{R}^{2}:\|x\|=1\right\}\), the unit circle in \(\mathrm{R}^{2}\). We apply Theorem 6.26 to describe \(S^{\prime}=\mathrm{T}(S)\).

Since \(T\) is invertible, it has rank equal to 2 and hence has singular values \(\sigma_{1} \geq \sigma_{2}>0\). Let \(\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}\right\}\) and \(\beta=\left\{u_{1}, u_{2}\right\}\) be orthonormal bases for \(\mathrm{R}^{2}\) so that \(\mathrm{T}\left(v_{1}\right)=\sigma_{1} u_{1}\) and \(\mathrm{T}\left(v_{2}\right)=\sigma_{2} u_{2}\), as in Theorem 6.26. Then \(\beta\) determines a coordinate system, which we shall call the \(x^{\prime} y^{\prime}\)-coordinate system for \(\mathrm{R}^{2}\), where the \(x^{\prime}\)-axis contains \(u_{1}\) and the \(y^{\prime}\)-axis contains \(u_{2}\). For any vector \(u \in \mathrm{R}^{2}\), if \(u=x_{1}^{\prime} u_{1}+x_{2}^{\prime} u_{2}\), then \([u]_{\beta}=\binom{x_{1}^{\prime}}{x_{2}^{\prime}}\) is the coordinate vector of \(u\) relative to \(\beta\). We characterize \(S^{\prime}\) in terms of an equation relating \(x_{1}^{\prime}\) and \(x_{2}^{\prime}\).

For any vector \(v=x_{1} v_{1}+x_{2} v_{2} \in \mathrm{R}^{2}\), the equation \(u=\mathrm{T}(v)\) means that

\[ u=\mathbf{T}\left(x_{1} v_{1}+x_{2} v_{2}\right)=x_{1} \mathbf{T}\left(v_{1}\right)+x_{2} \mathbf{T}\left(v_{2}\right)=x_{1} \sigma_{1} u_{1}+x_{2} \sigma_{2} u_{2} . \]

Thus for \(u=x_{1}^{\prime} u_{1}+x_{2}^{\prime} u_{2}\), we have \(x_{1}^{\prime}=x_{1} \sigma_{1}\) and \(x_{2}^{\prime}=x_{2} \sigma_{2}\). Furthermore, \(u \in S^{\prime}\) if and only if \(v \in S\) if and only if

\[ \frac{\left(x_{1}^{\prime}\right)^{2}}{\sigma_{1}^{2}}+\frac{\left(x_{2}^{\prime}\right)^{2}}{\sigma_{2}^{2}}=x_{1}^{2}+x_{2}^{2}=1 . \]

If \(\sigma_{1}=\sigma_{2}\), this is the equation of a circle of radius \(\sigma_{1}\), and if \(\sigma_{1}>\sigma_{2}\), this is the equation of an ellipse with major axis and minor axis oriented along the \(x^{\prime}\)-axis and the \(y^{\prime}\)-axis, respectively. (See Figure 6.6.)

Figure 6.6

The singular value theorem for linear transformations is useful in its matrix form because we can perform numerical computations on matrices. We begin with the definition of the singular values of a matrix.

Definition. Let \(A\) be an \(m \times n\) matrix. We define the singular values of \(A\) to be the singular values of the linear transformation \(L_{A}\).

Theorem 6.27 (Singular Value Decomposition Theorem for Matrices). Let \(A\) be an \(m \times n\) matrix of rank \(r\) with the positive singular values \(\sigma_{1} \geq \sigma_{2} \geq \cdots \geq \sigma_{r}\), and let \(\Sigma\) be the \(m \times n\) matrix defined by

\[ \Sigma_{i j}= \begin{cases}\sigma_{i} & \text { if } i=j \leq r \\ 0 & \text { otherwise }\end{cases} \]

Then there exists an \(m \times m\) unitary matrix \(U\) and an \(n \times n\) unitary matrix \(V\) such that

\[ A=U \Sigma V^{*} \]

Proof. Let \(T=\mathrm{L}_{A}: \mathrm{F}^{n} \rightarrow \mathrm{~F}^{m}\). By Theorem 6.26, there exist orthonormal bases \(\beta=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}\) for \(\mathrm{F}^{n}\) and \(\gamma=\left\{u_{1}, u_{2}, \ldots, u_{m}\right\}\) for \(\mathrm{F}^{m}\) such that \(\mathrm{T}\left(v_{i}\right)=\sigma_{i} u_{i}\) for \(1 \leq i \leq r\) and \(\mathrm{T}\left(v_{i}\right)=0\) for \(i>r\). Let \(U\) be the \(m \times m\) matrix whose \(j\) th column is \(u_{j}\) for all \(j\), and let \(V\) be the \(n \times n\) matrix whose \(j\) th column is \(v_{j}\) for all \(j\). Note that both \(U\) and \(V\) are unitary matrices.

By Theorem 2.13(a) (p. 90), the \(j\) th column of \(A V\) is \(A v_{j}=\sigma_{j} u_{j}\). Observe that the \(j\) th column of \(\Sigma\) is \(\sigma_{j} e_{j}\), where \(e_{j}\) is the \(j\) th standard vector of \(\mathrm{F}^{m}\). So by Theorem 2.13(a) and (b), the \(j\) th column of \(U \Sigma\) is given by

\[ U\left(\sigma_{j} e_{j}\right)=\sigma_{j} U\left(e_{j}\right)=\sigma_{j} u_{j} . \]

It follows that \(A V\) and \(U \Sigma\) are \(m \times n\) matrices whose corresponding columns are equal, and hence \(A V=U \Sigma\). Therefore \(A=A V V^{*}=U \Sigma V^{*}\).

Definition. Let \(A\) be an \(m \times n\) matrix of rank \(r\) with positive singular values \(\sigma_{1} \geq \sigma_{2} \geq \cdots \geq \sigma_{r}\). A factorization \(A=U \Sigma V^{*}\) where \(U\) and \(V\) are unitary matrices and \(\Sigma\) is the \(m \times n\) matrix defined as in Theorem 6.27 is called a singular value decomposition of \(A\).

In the proof of Theorem 6.27, the columns of \(V\) are the vectors in \(\beta\), and the columns of \(U\) are the vectors in \(\gamma\). Furthermore, the nonzero singular values of \(A\) are the same as those of \(L_{A}\); hence they are the square roots of the nonzero eigenvalues of \(A^{*} A\) or of \(A A^{*}\). (See Exercise 9.)

docs.title: Example 3
docs.text:
We find a singular value decomposition for \(A=\left(\begin{array}{lll}1 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 & -1\end{array}\right)\). First observe that for

\[ v_{1}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\left(\begin{array}{r} 1 \\ 1 \\ -1 \end{array}\right), \quad v_{2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left(\begin{array}{r} 1 \\ -1 \\ 0 \end{array}\right), \quad \text { and } \quad v_{3}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}\left(\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 1 \\ 2 \end{array}\right) \]

the set \(\beta=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, v_{3}\right\}\) is an orthonormal basis for \(\mathrm{R}^{3}\) consisting of eigenvectors of \(A^{*} A\) with corresponding eigenvalues \(\lambda_{1}=6\), and \(\lambda_{2}=\lambda_{3}=0\). Consequently, \(\sigma_{1}=\sqrt{6}\) is the only nonzero singular value of \(A\). Hence, as in the proof of Theorem 6.27, we let \(V\) be the matrix whose columns are the vectors in \(\beta\). Then

\[ \Sigma=\left(\begin{array}{ccc} \sqrt{6} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{array}\right) \quad \text { and } \quad V=\left(\begin{array}{ccc} \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} & \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \\ \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} & \frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}} & \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \\ \frac{-1}{\sqrt{3}} & 0 & \frac{2}{\sqrt{6}} \end{array}\right) \]

Also, as in Theorem 6.27, we take

\[ u_{1}=\frac{1}{\sigma_{i}} L_{A}\left(v_{1}\right)=\frac{1}{\sigma_{i}} A v_{1}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\binom{1}{1} . \]

Next choose \(u_{2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\binom{1}{-1}\), a unit vector orthogonal to \(u_{1}\), to obtain the orthonormal basis \(\gamma=\left\{u_{1}, u_{2}\right\}\) for \(\mathrm{R}^{2}\), and set

\[ U=\left(\begin{array}{ll} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & \frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}} \end{array}\right) . \]

Then \(A=U \Sigma V^{*}\) is the desired singular value decomposition.

docs.title: The Polar Decomposition of a Square Matrix
docs.text:
A singular value decomposition of a matrix can be used to factor a square matrix in a manner analogous to the factoring of a complex number as the product of a complex number of length 1 and a nonnegative number. In the case of matrices, the complex number of length 1 is replaced by a unitary matrix, and the nonnegative number is replaced by a positive semidefinite matrix.

Theorem 6.28 (Polar Decomposition). For any square matrix \(A\), there exists a unitary matrix \(W\) and a positive semidefinite matrix \(P\) such that

\[ A=W P \]

Furthermore, if \(A\) is invertible, then the representation is unique. Proof. By Theorem 6.27, there exist unitary matrices \(U\) and \(V\) and a diagonal matrix \(\Sigma\) with nonnegative diagonal entries such that \(A=U \Sigma V^{*}\). So

\[ A=U \Sigma V^{*}=U V^{*} V \Sigma V^{*}=W P \]

where \(W=U V^{*}\) and \(P=V \Sigma V^{*}\). Since \(W\) is the product of unitary matrices, \(W\) is unitary, and since \(\Sigma\) is positive semidefinite and \(P\) is unitarily equivalent to \(\Sigma, P\) is positive semidefinite by Exercise 14 of Section 6.5.

Now suppose that \(A\) is invertible and factors as the products

\[ A=W P=Z Q \]

where \(W\) and \(Z\) are unitary and \(P\) and \(Q\) are positive semidefinite. Since \(A\) is invertible, it follows that \(P\) and \(Q\) are positive definite and invertible, and therefore \(Z^{*} W=Q P^{-1}\). Thus \(Q P^{-1}\) is unitary, and so

\[ I=\left(Q P^{-1}\right)^{*}\left(Q P^{-1}\right)=P^{-1} Q^{2} P^{-1} \]

Hence \(P^{2}=Q^{2}\). Since both \(P\) and \(Q\) are positive definite, it follows that \(P=Q\) by Exercise 17 of Section 6.4. Thercfore \(W=Z\), and consequently the factorization is unique.

The factorization of a square matrix \(A\) as \(W P\) where \(W\) is unitary and \(P\) is positive semidefinite, is called a polar decomposition of \(A\).

docs.title: Example 4
docs.text:
To find the polar decomposition of \(A=\left(\begin{array}{rr}11 & -5 \\ -2 & 10\end{array}\right)\), we begin by finding a singular value decomposition \(U \Sigma V^{*}\) of \(A\). The object is to find an orthonormal basis \(\beta\) for \(\mathrm{R}^{2}\) consisting of eigenvectors of \(A^{*} A\). It can be shown that

\[ v_{1}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\binom{1}{-1} \quad \text { and } \quad v_{2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\binom{1}{1} \]

are orthonormal eigenvectors of \(A^{*} A\) with corrcsponding cigenvalucs \(\lambda_{1}=200\) and \(\lambda_{2}=50\). So \(\beta=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}\right\}\) is an appropriate basis. Thus \(\sigma_{1}=\sqrt{200}=\) \(10 \sqrt{2}\) and \(\sigma_{2}=\sqrt{50}=5 \sqrt{2}\) are the singular values of \(A\). So we have

\[ V=\left(\begin{array}{cc} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ \frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}} & \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \end{array}\right) \quad \text { and } \quad \Sigma=\left(\begin{array}{cc} 10 \sqrt{2} & 0 \\ 0 & 5 \sqrt{2} \end{array}\right) \]

Next, we find the columns \(u_{1}\) and \(u_{2}\) of \(U\) :

\[ u_{1}=\frac{1}{\sigma_{1}} A v_{1}=\frac{1}{5}\binom{4}{-3} \quad \text { and } \quad u_{2}=\frac{1}{\sigma_{2}} A v_{2}=\frac{1}{5}\binom{3}{4} . \]

Thus

\[ U=\left(\begin{array}{rr} \frac{4}{5} & \frac{3}{5} \\ -\frac{3}{5} & \frac{4}{5} \end{array}\right) \]

Therefore, in the notation of Theorem 6.28, we have

\[ W=U V^{*}=\left(\begin{array}{rr} \frac{4}{5} & \frac{3}{5} \\ -\frac{3}{5} & \frac{4}{5} \end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{cc} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & \frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \end{array}\right)=\frac{1}{5 \sqrt{2}}\left(\begin{array}{rr} 7 & -1 \\ 1 & 7 \end{array}\right) \]

and

\[ P=V \Sigma V^{*}=\left(\begin{array}{cc} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ \frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}} & \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{cc} 10 \sqrt{2} & 0 \\ 0 & 5 \sqrt{2} \end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{cc} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & \frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \end{array}\right)=\frac{5}{\sqrt{2}}\left(\begin{array}{rr} 3 & -1 \\ -1 & 3 \end{array}\right) \]

docs.title: The Pseudoinverse
docs.text:
Let V and W be finite-dimensional inner product spaces over the same field, and let \(\mathrm{T}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow \mathrm{W}\) be a linear transformation. It is desirable to have a linear transformation from \(W\) to \(V\) that captures some of the essence of an inverse of \(T\) even if \(T\) is not invertible. A simple approach to this problem is to focus on the "part" of T that is invertible, namely, the restriction of \(T\) to \(N(T)^{\perp}\). Let \(L: N(T)^{\perp} \rightarrow R(T)\) be the linear transformation defined by \(\mathrm{L}(x)=\mathrm{T}(x)\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~N}(T)^{\perp}\). Then L is invertible, and we can use the inverse of \(L\) to construct a linear transformation from \(W\) to \(V\) that salvages some of the benefits of an inverse of \(T\).

Definition. Let V and W be finite-dimensional inner product spaces over the same field, and let \(\mathrm{T}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow \mathrm{W}\) be a linear transformation. Let \(\mathrm{L}: \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})^{\perp} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}(\mathrm{T})\) be the linear transformation defined by \(\mathrm{L}(x)=\mathrm{T}(x)\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~N}(\mathrm{~T})^{\perp}\). The pseudoinverse (or Moore-Penrose generalized inverse) of T , denoted by \(T^{\dagger}\), is defined as the unique linear transformation from W to \(\checkmark\) such that

\[ \mathrm{T}^{\dagger}(y)= \begin{cases}\mathrm{L}^{-1}(y) & \text { for } y \in \mathrm{R}(\mathrm{~T}) \\ 0 & \text { for } y \in \mathrm{R}(\mathrm{~T})^{\perp}\end{cases} \]

The pseudoinverse of a linear transformation T on a finite-dimensional inner product space exists even if T is not invertible. Furthermore, if T is invertible, then \(\mathrm{T}^{\dagger}=\mathrm{T}^{-1}\) because \(\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})^{\perp}=\mathrm{V}\), and L (as just defined) coincides with \(T\).

As an extreme example, consider the zero transformation \(T_{0}: V \rightarrow W\) between two finite-dimensional inner product spaces \(V\) and \(W\). Then \(R\left(T_{0}\right)=\) \(\{0\}\), and therefore \(\mathrm{T}^{\dagger}\) is the zero transformation from W to V .

We can use the singular value theorem to describe the pseudoinverse of a linear transformation. Suppose that V and W are finite-dimensional vector spaces and \(\mathrm{T}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow \mathrm{W}\) is a linear transformation or rank \(r\). Let \(\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}\) and \(\left\{u_{1}, u_{2}, \ldots, u_{m}\right\}\) be orthonormal bases for \(V\) and \(W\), respectively, and let \(\sigma_{1} \geq \sigma_{2} \geq \cdots \geq \sigma_{r}\) be the nonzero singular values of T satisfying (4) in Theorem 6.26. Then \(\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{r}\right\}\) is a basis for \(\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})^{\perp},\left\{v_{r+1}, v_{r+2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}\) is a basis for \(\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T}),\left\{u_{1}, u_{2}, \ldots, u_{r}\right\}\) is a basis for \(\mathrm{R}(\mathrm{T})\), and \(\left\{u_{r+1}, u_{r+2}, \ldots, u_{m}\right\}\) is a basis for \(R(T)^{\perp}\). Let \(L\) be the restriction of \(T\) to \(N(T)^{\perp}\), as in the definition of pseudoinverse. Then \(\mathrm{L}^{-1}\left(u_{i}\right)=\frac{1}{\sigma_{i}} v_{i}\) for \(1 \leq i \leq r\). Therefore

\[ \mathrm{T}^{\dagger}\left(u_{i}\right)= \begin{cases}\frac{1}{\sigma_{i}} v_{i} & \text { if } 1 \leq i \leq r \tag{6}\\ 0 & \text { if } r<i \leq m\end{cases} \]

docs.title: Example 5
docs.text:
Let \(\mathrm{T}: \mathrm{P}_{2}(R) \rightarrow \mathrm{P}_{1}(R)\) be the linear transformation defined by \(\mathrm{T}(f(x))=\) \(f^{\prime}(x)\), as in Example 1. Let \(\beta=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, v_{3}\right\}\) and \(\gamma=\left\{u_{1}, u_{2}\right\}\) be the orthonormal bases for \(\mathrm{P}_{2}(R)\) and \(\mathrm{P}_{1}(R)\) in Example 1. Then \(\sigma_{1}=\sqrt{15}\) and \(\sigma_{2}=\sqrt{3}\) are the nonzero singular values of T . It follows that

\[ \mathrm{T}^{\dagger}\left(\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} x\right)=\mathrm{T}^{\dagger}\left(u_{1}\right)=\frac{1}{\sigma_{1}} v_{1}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{15}} \sqrt{\frac{5}{8}}\left(3 x^{2}-1\right) \]

and hence

\[ \mathrm{T}^{\dagger}(x)=\frac{1}{6}\left(3 x^{2}-1\right) \]

Similarly, \(\mathrm{T}^{\dagger}(1)=x\). Thus, for any polynomial \(a+b x \in \mathrm{P}_{1}(R)\),

\[ \mathrm{T}^{\dagger}(a+b x)=a \top^{\dagger}(1)+b \mathrm{~T}^{\dagger}(x)=a x+\frac{b}{6}\left(3 x^{2}-1\right) \]

docs.title: The Pseudoinverse of a Matrix
docs.text:
Let \(A\) be an \(m \times n\) matrix. Then there cxists a unique \(n \times m\) matrix \(B\) such that \(\left(\mathrm{L}_{A}\right)^{\dagger}: \mathrm{F}^{m} \rightarrow \mathrm{~F}^{n}\) is equal to the left-multiplication transformation \(\mathrm{L}_{B}\). We call \(B\) the pseudoinverse of \(A\) and denote it by \(B=A^{\dagger}\). Thus

\[ \left(\mathrm{L}_{A}\right)^{\dagger}=\mathrm{L}_{A^{\dagger}} . \]

Let \(A\) be an \(m \times n\) matrix of rank \(r\). The pseudoinverse of \(A\) can be computed with the aid of a singular value decomposition \(A=U \Sigma V^{*}\). Let \(\beta\) and \(\gamma\) be the ordered bases whose vectors are the columns of \(V\) and \(U\), respectively, and let \(\sigma_{1} \geq \sigma_{2} \geq \cdots \geq \sigma_{r}\) be the nonzero singular values of \(A\). Then \(\beta\) and \(\gamma\) are orthonormal bases for \(\mathrm{F}^{n}\) and \(\mathrm{F}^{m}\), respectively, and (4) and (6) are satisfied for \(\mathrm{T}=\mathrm{L}_{A}\). Reversing the roles of \(\beta\) and \(\gamma\) in the proof of Theorem 6.27, we obtain the following result.

Theorem 6.29. Let \(A\) be an \(m \times n\) matrix of rank \(r\) with a singular value decomposition \(A=U \Sigma V^{*}\) and nonzero singular values \(\sigma_{1} \geq \sigma_{2} \geq \cdots \geq \sigma_{r}\). Let \(\Sigma^{\dagger}\) be the \(n \times m\) matrix defined by

\[ \Sigma_{i j}^{\dagger}= \begin{cases}\frac{1}{\sigma_{i}} & \text { if } i=j \leq r \\ 0 & \text { otherwise }\end{cases} \]

Then \(A^{\dagger}=V \Sigma^{\dagger} U^{*}\), and this is a singular value decomposition of \(A^{\dagger}\). Notice that \(\Sigma^{\dagger}\) as defined in Theorem 6.29 is actually the pseudoinverse of \(\Sigma\).

docs.title: Example 6
docs.text:
We find \(A^{\dagger}\) for the matrix \(A=\left(\begin{array}{lll}1 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 & -1\end{array}\right)\). Since \(A\) is the matrix of Example 3, we can use the singular value decomposition obtained in that example:

\[ A=U \Sigma V^{*}=\left(\begin{array}{cc} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & \frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}} \end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{ccc} \sqrt{6} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{ccc} \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} & \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \\ \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} & \frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}} & \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \\ \frac{-1}{\sqrt{3}} & 0 & \frac{2}{\sqrt{6}} \end{array}\right)^{*} . \]

By Theorem 6.29, we have

\[ A^{\dagger}=V \Sigma^{\dagger} U^{*}=\left(\begin{array}{ccc} \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} & \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \\ \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} & \frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}} & \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \\ \frac{-1}{\sqrt{3}} & 0 & \frac{2}{\sqrt{6}} \end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{cc} \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{ll} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & \frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}} \end{array}\right)=\frac{1}{6}\left(\begin{array}{rr} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \\ -1 & -1 \end{array}\right) . \]

Notice that the linear transformation T of Example 5 is \(\mathrm{L}_{A}\), where \(A\) is the matrix of Example 6, and that \(T^{\dagger}=L_{A^{\dagger}}\).

docs.title: The Pseudoinverse and Systems of Linear Equations
docs.text:
Let \(A\) be an \(m \times n\) matrix with entries in \(F\). Then for any \(b \in \mathrm{~F}^{m}\), the matrix equation \(A x=b\) is a system of linear equations, and so it either has no solutions, a unique solution, or infinitely many solutions. We know that the system has a unique solution for every \(b \in \mathrm{~F}^{m}\) if and only if \(A\) is invertible, in which case the solution is given by \(A^{-1} b\). Furthermore, if \(A\) is invertible, then \(A^{-1}=A^{\dagger}\), and so the solution can be written as \(x=A^{\dagger} b\). If, on the other hand, \(A\) is not invertible or the system \(A x=b\) is inconsistent, then \(A^{\dagger} b\) still exists. We thercfore pose the following question: In general, how is the vector \(A^{\dagger} b\) related to the system of linear equations \(A x=b\) ?

In order to answer this question, we need the following lemma. Lemma. Let \(V\) and \(W\) be finite-dimensional inner product spaces, and let \(\mathrm{T}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow \mathrm{W}\) be lincar. Then (a) \(\mathrm{T}^{\dagger} \mathrm{T}\) is the orthogonal projection of V on \(\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})^{\perp}\). (b) \(\mathrm{TT}^{\dagger}\) is the orthogonal projection of W on \(\mathrm{R}(\mathrm{T})\).

Proof. As in the earlier discussion, we define \(\mathrm{L}: \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})^{\perp} \rightarrow \mathrm{W}\) by \(\mathrm{L}(x)=\) \(\mathrm{T}(x)\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~N}(\mathrm{~T})^{\perp}\). If \(x \in \mathrm{~N}(\mathrm{~T})^{\perp}\), then \(\mathrm{T}^{\dagger} \mathrm{T}(x)=\mathrm{L}^{-1} \mathrm{~L}(x)=x\), and if \(x \in \mathrm{~N}(\mathrm{~T})\), then \(\mathrm{T}^{\dagger} \mathrm{T}(x)=\mathrm{T}^{\dagger}(0)=0\). Consequently \(\mathrm{T}^{\dagger} \mathrm{T}\) is the orthogonal projection of V on \(\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})^{\perp}\). This proves (a).

The proof of (b) is similar and is left as an exercise. Theorem 6.30. Consider the system of linear equations \(A x=b\), where \(A\) is an \(m \times n\) matrix and \(b \in \mathrm{~F}^{m}\). If \(z=A^{\dagger} b\), then \(z\) has the following properties. (a) If \(A x=b\) is consistent, then \(z\) is the unique solution to the system having minimum norm. That is, \(z\) is a solution to the system, and if \(y\) is any solution to the system, then \(\|z\| \leq\|y\|\) with equality if and only if \(z=y\). (b) If \(A x=b\) is inconsistent, then \(z\) is the unique best approximation to a solution having minimum norm. That is, \(\|A z-b\| \leq\|A y-b\|\) for any \(y \in \mathrm{~F}^{n}\), with equality if and only if \(A z=A y\). Furthermore, if \(A z=A y\), then \(\|z\| \leq\|y\|\) with equality if and only if \(z=y\).

Proof. For convenience, let \(T=L_{A}\). (a) Suppose that \(A x=b\) is consistent, and let \(z=A^{\dagger} b\). Observe that \(b \in \mathrm{R}(\mathrm{T})\), and therefore \(A z=A A^{\dagger} b=\mathrm{TT}^{\dagger}(b)=b\) by part (b) of the lemma. Thus \(z\) is a solution to the system. Now suppose that \(y\) is any solution to the system. Then

\[ \mathrm{T}^{\dagger} \mathrm{T}(y)=A^{\dagger} A y=A^{\dagger} b=z \]

and hence \(z\) is the orthogonal projection of \(y\) on \(N(T)^{\perp}\) by part (a) of the lemma. Therefore, by the corollary to Theorem 6.6 (p. 350), we have that \(\|z\| \leq\|y\|\) with equality if and only if \(z=y\). (b) Suppose that \(A x=b\) is incousistent. By the lemma. \(A z=A A^{\dagger} b=\) \(\mathrm{TT}^{\dagger}(b)=b\) is the orthogonal projection of \(b\) on \(\mathrm{R}(\mathrm{T})\); therefore, by the corollary to Theorem 6.6 (p.350), \(A z\) is the vector in \(\mathrm{R}(\mathrm{T})\) nearest \(b\). That is, if \(A y\) is any other vector in \(\mathrm{R}(\mathrm{T})\), then \(\|A z-b\| \leq\|A y-b\|\) with equality if and only if \(A z=A y\).

Finally, suppose that \(y\) is any vector in \(\mathrm{F}^{n}\) such that \(A z=A y=c\). Then

\[ A^{\dagger} c=A^{\dagger} A z=A^{\dagger} A A^{\dagger} b=A^{\dagger} b=z \]

by Exercise 23; hence we may apply part (a) of this theorem to the system \(A x=c\) to conclude that \(\|z\| \leq\|y\|\) with equality if and only if \(z=y\).

Note that the vector \(z=A^{\dagger} b\) in Theorem 6.30 is the vector \(x_{0}\) described in Theorem 6.12 that arises in the least squares application on pages 360-364.

docs.title: Example 7
docs.text:
Consider the linear systems

\[ \begin{aligned} & x_{1}+x_{2}-x_{3}=1 \\ & x_{1}+x_{2}-x_{3}=1 \end{aligned} \quad \text { and } \quad \begin{aligned} & x_{1}+x_{2}-x_{3}=1 \\ & x_{1}+x_{2}-x_{3}=2 . \end{aligned} \]

The first system has infinitely many solutions. Let \(A=\left(\begin{array}{lll}1 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 & -1\end{array}\right)\), the coefficient matrix of the system, and let \(b=\binom{1}{1}\). By Example 6,

\[ A^{\dagger}=\frac{1}{6}\left(\begin{array}{rr} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \\ -1 & -1 \end{array}\right) \]

and therefore

\[ z=A^{\dagger} b=\frac{1}{6}\left(\begin{array}{rr} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \\ -1 & -1 \end{array}\right)\binom{1}{1}=\frac{1}{3}\left(\begin{array}{r} 1 \\ 1 \\ -1 \end{array}\right) \]

is the solution of minimal norm by Theorem 6.30(a). The second system is obviously inconsistent. Let \(b=\binom{1}{2}\). Thus, although

\[ z=A^{\dagger} b=\frac{1}{6}\left(\begin{array}{rr} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \\ -1 & -1 \end{array}\right)\binom{1}{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\begin{array}{r} 1 \\ 1 \\ -1 \end{array}\right) \]

is not a solution to the second system, it is the "best approximation" to a solution having minimum norm, as described in Theorem 6.30(b).

docs.title: EXERCISES
docs.text:
1. Label the following statements as true or false. (a) The singular values of any linear operator on a finite-dimensional vector space are also eigenvalues of the operator. (b) The singular values of any matrix \(A\) are the eigenvalues of \(A^{*} A\). (c) For any matrix \(A\) and any scalar \(c\), if \(\sigma\) is a singular value of \(A\), then \(|c| \sigma\) is a singular value of \(c A\). (d) The singular values of any linear operator are nonnegative. (e) If \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of a self-adjoint matrix \(A\), then \(\lambda\) is a singular value of \(A\). (f) For any \(m \times n\) matrix \(A\) and any \(b \in \mathrm{~F}^{n}\), the vector \(A^{\dagger} b\) is a solution to \(A x=b\). (g) The pseudoinverse of any linear operator exists even if the operator is not invertible.
  2. Let \(\mathrm{T}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow \mathrm{W}\) be a linear transformation of rank \(r\), where V and W are finite-dimensional inner product spaces. In each of the following, find orthonormal bases \(\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}\) for V and \(\left\{u_{1}, u_{2}, \ldots, u_{m}\right\}\) for \(W\), and the nonzero singular values \(\sigma_{1} \geq \sigma_{2} \geq \cdots \geq \sigma_{r}\) of \(T\) such that \(\mathrm{T}\left(v_{i}\right)=\sigma_{i} u_{i}\) for \(1 \leq i \leq r\). (a) \(\mathrm{T}: \mathrm{R}^{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}^{3}\) defined by \(\mathrm{T}\left(x_{1}, x_{2}\right)=\left(x_{1}, x_{1}+x_{2}, x_{1}-x_{2}\right)\) (b) \(\mathrm{T}: \mathrm{P}_{2}(R) \rightarrow \mathrm{P}_{1}(R)\), where \(\mathrm{T}(f(x))=f^{\prime \prime}(x)\), and the inner products are defined as in Example 1 (c) Let \(\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{W}=\operatorname{span}(\{1, \sin x, \cos x\})\) with the inner product defined by \(\langle f, g\rangle=\int_{0}^{2 \pi} f(t) g(t) d t\), and T is defined by \(\mathrm{T}(f)=f^{\prime}+2 f\) (d) \(\mathrm{T}: \mathrm{C}^{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}^{2}\) defined by \(\mathrm{T}\left(z_{1}, z_{2}\right)=\left((1-i) z_{2},(1+i) z_{1}+z_{2}\right)\)
  3. Find a singular value decomposition for each of the following matrices. (a) \(\left(\begin{array}{rr}1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \\ -1 & -1\end{array}\right)\) (b) \(\left(\begin{array}{rrr}1 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & -1\end{array}\right) \quad\) (c) \(\left(\begin{array}{ll}1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1\end{array}\right)\) (d) \(\left(\begin{array}{rrr}1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & -1\end{array}\right)\) (e) \(\left(\begin{array}{cc}1+i & 1 \\ 1-i & -i\end{array}\right)\) (f) \(\left(\begin{array}{rrrr}1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & -2 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 & 1 & 1\end{array}\right)\)
  4. Find a polar decomposition for each of the following matrices. (a) \(\left(\begin{array}{rr}1 & 1 \\ 2 & -2\end{array}\right)\) (b) \(\left(\begin{array}{rrr}20 & 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 4 & 20 & 0\end{array}\right)\)
  5. Find an explicit formula for each of the following expressions. (a) \(\mathrm{T}^{\dagger}\left(x_{1}, x_{2}, x_{3}\right)\), where T is the linear transformation of Exercise 2(a) (b) \(\mathrm{T}^{\dagger}\left(a+b x+c x^{2}\right)\), where T is the linear transformation of Exercise 2(b) (c) \(\mathrm{T}^{\dagger}(a+b \sin x+c \cos x)\), where T is the linear transformation of Exercise 2(c) (d) \(\mathrm{T}^{\dagger}\left(z_{1}, z_{2}\right)\), where T is the linear transformation of Exercise 2(d)
  6. Use the results of Exercise 3 to find the pseudoinverse of each of the following matrices. (a) \(\left(\begin{array}{rr}1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \\ -1 & -1\end{array}\right)\) (b) \(\left(\begin{array}{rrr}1 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & -1\end{array}\right)\) (c) \(\left(\begin{array}{ll}1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1\end{array}\right)\) (d) \(\left(\begin{array}{rrr}1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & -1\end{array}\right)\) (e) \(\left(\begin{array}{cc}1+i & 1 \\ 1-i & -i\end{array}\right)\) (f) \(\left(\begin{array}{rrrr}1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & -2 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 & 1 & 1\end{array}\right)\)
  7. For each of the given linear transformations \(T: V \rightarrow W\), (i) Describe the subspace \(Z_{1}\) of \(V\) such that \(T^{\dagger} T\) is the orthogonal projection of \(V\) on \(Z_{1}\). (ii) Describe the subspace \(\mathrm{Z}_{2}\) of W such that \(\mathrm{TT}^{\dagger}\) is the orthogonal projection of \(W\) on \(Z_{2}\). (a) T is the linear transformation of Exercise 2(a) (b) T is the linear transformation of Exercise 2(b) (c) T is the linear transformation of Exercise 2(c) (d) T is the linear transformation of Exercise 2(d)
  8. For each of the given systems of linear equations, (i) If the system is consistent, find the unique solution having minimum norm. (ii) If the system is inconsistent, find the "best approximation to a solution". having minimum norm, as described in Theorem 6.30(b). (Use your answers to parts (a) and (f) of Exercise 6.) (a)
\[ \begin{array}{r} x_{1}+x_{2}=1 \\ x_{1}+x_{2}=2 \\ -x_{1}+-x_{2}=0 \end{array} \] \[ \text { (b) } \begin{aligned} & x_{1}+x_{2}+x_{3}+x_{4}=2 \\ & x_{1}-2 x_{3}+x_{4}=-1 \\ & x_{1}-x_{2}+x_{3}+x_{4}=2 \end{aligned} \]
  1. Let V and W be finite-dimensional inner product spaces over \(F\), and suppose that \(\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}\) and \(\left\{u_{1}, u_{2}, \ldots, u_{m}\right\}\) are orthonormal bases for V and W , respectively. Let \(\mathrm{T}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow \mathrm{W}\) is a linear transformation of rank \(r\), and suppose that \(\sigma_{1} \geq \sigma_{2} \geq \cdots \geq \sigma_{\tau}>0\) are such that
\[ \mathrm{T}\left(v_{i}\right)= \begin{cases}\sigma_{i} u_{i} & \text { if } 1 \leq i \leq r \\ 0 & \text { if } r<i\end{cases} \]

(a) Prove that \(\left\{u_{1}, u_{2}, \ldots, u_{m}\right\}\) is a set of eigenvectors of TT* with corresponding eigenvalues \(\lambda_{1}, \lambda_{2}, \ldots, \lambda_{m}\), where

\[ \lambda_{i}= \begin{cases}\sigma_{i}^{2} & \text { if } 1 \leq i \leq r \\ 0 & \text { if } r<i\end{cases} \]

(b) Let \(A\) be an \(m \times n\) matrix with real or complex entries. Prove that the nonzero singular values of \(A\) are the positive square roots of the nonzero eigenvalucs of \(A A^{*}\), including repetitions. (c) Prove that TT and TT have the same nonzero eigenvalues, including repetitions. (d) State and prove a result for matrices analogous to (c). 10. Use Exercise 8 of Section 2.5 to obtain another proof of Theorem 6.27, the singular value decomposition theorem for matrices. 11. This exercise relates the singular values of a well-behaved linear operator or matrix to its eigenvalues. (a) Let T be a normal linear operator on an \(n\)-dimensional inner product space with cigenvalues \(\lambda_{1}, \lambda_{2}, \ldots, \lambda_{n}\). Prove that the singular values of T are \(\left|\lambda_{1}\right|,\left|\lambda_{2}\right|, \ldots,\left|\lambda_{n}\right|\). (b) State and prove a result for matrices analogous to (a). 12. Let \(A\) be a normal matrix with an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors \(\beta=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}\) and corresponding eigenvalues \(\lambda_{1}, \lambda_{2}, \ldots, \lambda_{n}\). Let \(V\) be the \(n \times n\) matrix whose columns are the vectors in \(\beta\). Prove that for each \(i\) there is a scalar \(\theta_{i}\) of absolute value 1 such that if \(U\) is the \(n \times n\) matrix with \(\theta_{i} v_{i}\) as column \(i\) and \(\Sigma\) is the diagonal matrix such that \(\Sigma_{i i}=\left|\lambda_{i}\right|\) for each \(i\), then \(U \Sigma V^{*}\) is a singular value decomposition of \(A\). 13. Prove that if \(A\) is a positive semidefinite matrix, then the singular values of \(A\) are the same as the cigenvalues of \(A\). 14. Prove that if \(A\) is a positive definite matrix and \(A=U \Sigma V^{*}\) is a singular value decomposition of \(A\), then \(U=V\). 15. Let \(A\) be a square matrix with a polar decomposition \(A=W P\). (a) Prove that \(A\) is normal if and only if \(W P^{2}=P^{2} W\). (b) Use (a) to prove that \(A\) is normal if and only if \(W P=P W\). 16. Let \(A\) be a square matrix. Prove an alternate form of the polar decomposition for \(A\) : There exists a unitary matrix \(W\) and a positive semidefinite matrix \(P\) such that \(A=P W\). 17. Let \(T\) and \(\mathbf{U}\) be linear operators on \(\mathbf{R}^{2}\) defined for all \(\left(x_{1}, x_{2}\right) \in \mathbf{R}^{2}\) by

\[ \mathrm{T}\left(x_{1}, x_{2}\right)=\left(x_{1}, 0\right) \text { and } \mathrm{U}\left(x_{1}, x_{2}\right)=\left(x_{1}+x_{2}, 0\right) \]

(a) Prove that (UT) \()^{\dagger} \neq T^{\dagger} U^{\dagger}\). (b) Exhibit matrices \(A\) and \(B\) such that \(A B\) is defined, but \((A B)^{\dagger} \neq\) \(B^{\dagger} A^{\dagger}\). 18. Let \(A\) be an \(m \times n\) matrix. Prove the following results. (a) For any \(m \times m\) unitary matrix \(G,(G A)^{\dagger}=A^{\dagger} G^{*}\). (b) For any \(n \times n\) unitary matrix \(H,(A H)^{\dagger}=H^{*} A^{\dagger}\). 19. Let \(A\) be a matrix with real or complex entries. Prove the following results. (a) The nonzero singular values of \(A\) are the same as the nonzero singular values of \(A^{*}\), which are the same as the nonzero singular values of \(A^{t}\). (b) \(\left(A^{\dagger}\right)^{*}=\left(A^{*}\right)^{\dagger}\). (c) \(\left(A^{\dagger}\right)^{t}=\left(A^{t}\right)^{\dagger}\). 20. Let \(A\) be a square matrix such that \(A^{2}=O\). Prove that \(\left(A^{\dagger}\right)^{2}=O\). 21. Let \(V\) and \(W\) be finite-dimensional inner product spaces, and let \(T: V \rightarrow W\) be linear. Prove the following results. (a) \(\mathrm{TT}^{\dagger} \mathrm{T}=\mathrm{T}\). (b) \(\mathrm{T}^{\dagger} \mathrm{TT}^{\dagger}=\mathrm{T}^{\dagger}\). (c) Both \(\mathrm{T}^{\dagger} \mathrm{T}\) and \(\mathrm{TT}^{\dagger}\) are self-adjoint.

The preceding three statements are called the Penrose conditions, and they characterize the pseudoinverse of a linear transformation as shown in Exercise 22. 22. Let \(V\) and \(W\) be finite-dimensional inner product spaces. Let \(T: V \rightarrow W\) and \(\dot{U}: W \rightarrow V\) be linear transformations such that \(T U T=T, U T U=U\), and both UT and TU are self-adjoint. Prove that \(U=T^{\dagger}\). 23. State and prove a result for matrices that is analogous to the result of Exercise 21. 24. State and prove a result for matrices that is analogous to the result of Exercise 22. 25. Let \(V\) and \(W\) be finite-dimensional inner product spaces, and let \(\mathrm{T}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow \mathrm{W}\) be linear. Prove the following results. (a) If \(T\) is one-to-one, then \(T^{*} T\) is invertible and \(T^{\dagger}=\left(T^{*} T\right)^{-1} T^{*}\). (b) If T is onto, then \(\mathrm{TT}^{*}\) is invertible and \(\mathrm{T}^{\dagger}=\mathrm{T}^{*}\left(\mathrm{TT}^{*}\right)^{-1}\). 26. Let V and W be finite-dimensional inner product spaces with orthonormal bases \(\beta\) and \(\gamma\), respectively, and let \(\mathrm{T}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow \mathrm{W}\) be linear. Prove that \(\left([\mathrm{T}]_{\beta}^{\gamma}\right)^{\dagger}=\left[\mathrm{T}^{\dagger}\right]_{\gamma}^{\beta}\). 27. Let \(V\) and \(W\) be finite-dimensional inner product spaces, and let \(\mathrm{T}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow \mathrm{W}\) be a linear transformation. Prove part (b) of the lemma to Theorem 6.30: \(\mathrm{TT}^{\dagger}\) is the orthogonal projection of \(W\) on \(R(T)\).

docs.title: 6.8* BILINEAR AND QUADRATIC FORMS
docs.text: There is a certain class of scalar-valued functions of two variables defined on a vector space that arises in the study of such diverse subjects as geometry and multivariable calculus. This is the class of bilinear forms. We study the basic properties of this class with a special emphasis on symmetric bilinear forms, and we consider some of its applications to quadratic surfaces and multivariable calculus.

docs.title: Bilinear Forms
docs.text:
Definition. Let \(\vee\) be a vector space over a field \(F\). A function \(H\) from the set \(\mathrm{V} \times \mathrm{V}\) of ordered pairs of vectors to \(F\) is called a bilinear form on V if \(H\) is lincar in each variable when the other variable is held fixed; that is, \(H\) is a bilinear form on V if (a) \(H\left(a x_{1}+x_{2}, y\right)=a H\left(x_{1}, y\right)+H\left(x_{2}, y\right) \quad\) for all \(x_{1}, x_{2}, y \in \mathrm{~V}\) and \(a \in F\) (b) \(H\left(x, a y_{1}+y_{2}\right)=a H\left(x, y_{1}\right)+H\left(x, y_{2}\right) \quad\) for all \(x, y_{1}, y_{2} \in \mathrm{~V}\) and \(a \in F\).

We denote the set of all bilinear forms on V by \(\mathcal{B}(\mathrm{V})\). Observe that an inner product on a vector space is a bilinear form if the underlying field is real, but not if the underlying field is complex.

docs.title: Example 1
docs.text:
Define a function \(H: \mathrm{R}^{2} \times \mathrm{R}^{2} \rightarrow R\) by

\[ H\left(\binom{a_{1}}{a_{2}},\binom{b_{1}}{b_{2}}\right)=2 a_{1} b_{1}+3 a_{1} b_{2}+4 a_{2} b_{1}-a_{2} b_{2} \quad \text { for }\binom{a_{1}}{a_{2}},\binom{b_{1}}{b_{2}} \in \mathrm{R}^{2} \]

We could verify directly that \(H\) is a bilinear form on \(\mathrm{R}^{2}\). However, it is more enlightening and less tedious to observe that if

\[ A=\left(\begin{array}{rr} 2 & 3 \\ 4 & -1 \end{array}\right), \quad x=\binom{a_{1}}{a_{2}}, \quad \text { and } \quad y=\binom{b_{1}}{b_{2}} \]

then

\[ H(x, y)=x^{t} A y \]

The bilinearity of \(H\) now follows directly from the distributive property of matrix multiplication over matrix addition.

The preceding bilinear form is a special case of the next example.

docs.title: Example 2
docs.text:
Let \(\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{F}^{n}\), where the vectors are considered as column vectors. For any \(A \in \mathrm{M}_{n \times n}(F)\), define \(H: \vee \times \vee \rightarrow F\) by

\[ H(x, y)=x^{t} A y \quad \text { for } x, y \in \mathrm{~V} . \]

Notice that since \(x\) and \(y\) are \(n \times 1\) matrices and \(A\) is an \(n \times n\) matrix, \(H(x, y)\) is a \(1 \times 1\) matrix. We identify this matrix with its single entry. The bilinearity of \(H\) follows as in Example 1. For example, for \(a \in F\) and \(x_{1}, x_{2}, y \in \mathrm{~V}\), we have

\[ \begin{aligned} H\left(a x_{1}+x_{2}, y\right) & =\left(a x_{1}+x_{2}\right)^{t} A y=\left(a x_{1}^{t}+x_{2}^{t}\right) A y \\ & =a x_{1}^{t} A y+x_{2}^{t} A y \\ & =a H\left(x_{1}, y\right)+H\left(x_{2}, y\right) \end{aligned} \]

We list several properties possessed by all bilinear forms. Their proofs are left to the reader (see Excrcise 2).

For any bilinear form \(H\) on a vector space \(\vee\) over a field \(F\), the following propertiès hold.

  1. If, for any \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\), the functions \(\mathrm{L}_{x}, \mathrm{R}_{x}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow F\) are defined by
\[ \mathrm{L}_{x}(y)=H(x, y) \quad \text { and } \quad \mathrm{R}_{x}(y)=H(y, x) \quad \text { for all } y \in \mathrm{~V} \]

then \(\mathrm{L}_{x}\) and \(\mathrm{R}_{x}\) are linear. 2. \(H(0, x)=H(x, 0)=0\) for all \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\). 3. For all \(x, y, z, w \in \mathrm{~V}\),

\[ H(x+y, z+w)=H(x, z)+H(x, w)+H(y, z)+H(y, w) \]
  1. If \(J: \vee \times \mathrm{V} \rightarrow F\) is defined by \(J(x, y)=H(y, x)\), then \(J\) is a bilinear form.

Definitions. Let \(V\) be a vector space, let \(H_{1}\) and \(H_{2}\) be bilinear forms on V , and let \(a\) be a scalar. We define the sum \(H_{1}+H_{2}\) and the scalar product \(a H_{1}\) by the equations

\[ \left(H_{1}+H_{2}\right)(x, y)=H_{1}(x, y)+H_{2}(x, y) \]

and

\[ \left(a H_{1}\right)(\dot{x}, y)=a\left(H_{1}(x, y)\right) \quad \text { for all } x, y \in \mathrm{~V} \]

The following theorem is an immediate consequence of the definitions.

Theorem 6.31. For any vector space V , the sum of two bilinear forms and the product of a scalar and a bilinear form on \(V\) are again bilincar forms on V . Furthermore, \(\mathcal{B}(\mathrm{V})\) is a vector space with respect to these operations.

Proof. Exercise. Let \(\beta=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}\) be an ordered basis for an \(n\)-dimensional vector space V , and let \(H \in \mathcal{B}(\mathrm{~V})\). We can associate with \(H\) an \(n \times n\) matrix \(A\) whose entry in row \(i\) and column \(j\) is defined by

\[ A_{i j}=H\left(v_{i}, v_{j}\right) \quad \text { for } i, j=1,2, \ldots, n \]

Definition. The matrix \(A\) above is called the matrix representation of \(H\) with respect to the ordered basis \(\beta\) and is denoted by \(\psi_{\beta}(H)\).

We can therefore regard \(\psi_{\beta}\) as a mapping from \(\mathcal{B}(V)\) to \(M_{n \times n}(F)\), where \(F\) is the field of scalars for V , that takes a bilincar form \(H\) into its matrix representation \(\psi_{\beta}(H)\). We first consider an example and then show that \(\psi_{\beta}\) is an isomorphism.

docs.title: Example 3
docs.text:
Consider the bilinear form \(H\) of Example 1, and let.

\[ \beta=\left\{\binom{1}{1},\binom{1}{-1}\right\} \quad \text { and } \quad B=\psi_{B}(H) . \]

Then

\[ \begin{aligned} & B_{11}=H\left(\binom{1}{1} \cdot\binom{1}{1}\right)=2+3+4-1=8 \\ & B_{12}=H\left(\binom{1}{1} \cdot\binom{1}{-1}\right)=2-3+4+1=4 \\ & B_{21}=H\left(\binom{1}{-1} \cdot\binom{1}{1}\right)=2+3-4+1=2 \end{aligned} \]

and

\[ B_{22}=H\left(\binom{1}{-1},\binom{1}{-1}\right)=2-3-4-1=-6 . \]

So

\[ \psi_{\beta}(H)=\left(\begin{array}{rr} 8 & 4 \\ 2 & -6 \end{array}\right) . \]

If \(\gamma\) is the standard ordered basis for \(\mathrm{R}^{2}\), the reader can verify that

\[ \psi_{\gamma}(H)=\left(\begin{array}{rr} 2 & 3 \\ 4 & -1 \end{array}\right) \]

Theorem 6.32. For any \(n\)-dimensional vector space \(V\) over \(F\) and any ordered basis \(\beta\) for \(\mathrm{V}, \psi_{\beta}: \mathcal{B}(\mathrm{V}) \rightarrow \mathrm{M}_{n \times n}(F)\) is an isomorphism.

Proof. We leave the proof that \(\psi_{\beta}\) is linear to the reader. To show that \(\psi_{\beta}\) is one-to-onc, suppose that \(\psi_{\beta}(H)=O\) for some \(H \in\) \(\mathcal{B}(\mathrm{V})\). Fix \(v_{i} \in \beta\), and recall the mapping \(L_{v_{i}}: V \rightarrow F\), which is linear by property 1 on page 423 . By hypothesis, \(\mathrm{L}_{v_{i}}\left(v_{j}\right)=H\left(v_{i}, v_{j}\right)=0\) for all \(v_{j} \in \beta\). Hence \(\mathrm{L}_{v_{i}}\) is the zero transformation from V to \(F\). So

\[ \begin{equation*} H\left(v_{i}, x\right)=\mathrm{L}_{v_{i}}(x)=0 \quad \text { for all } x \in \mathrm{~V} \text { and } v_{i} \in \beta \tag{7} \end{equation*} \]

Next fix an arbitrary \(y \in \mathrm{~V}\), and recall the lincar mapping \(\mathrm{R}_{y}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow F\) defined in property 1 on page 423 . By (7), \(\mathrm{R}_{y}\left(v_{i}\right)=H\left(v_{i}, y\right)=0\) for all \(v_{i} \in \beta\), and hence \(\mathrm{R}_{y}\) is the zero transformation. So \(H(x, y)=\mathrm{R}_{y}(x)=0\) for all \(x, y \in \mathrm{~V}\). Thus \(H\) is the zero bilinear form, and therefore \(\psi_{\beta}\) is one-to-onc.

To show that \(\psi_{\beta}\) is onto, consider any \(A \in \mathrm{M}_{n \times n}(F)\). Recall the isomorphism \(\phi_{\beta}: \mathrm{V} \rightarrow \mathrm{F}^{n}\) defined in Section 2.4. For \(x \in \mathrm{~V}\), we view \(\phi_{\beta}(x) \in \mathrm{F}^{n}\) as a column vector. Let \(H: \vee \times \mathrm{V} \rightarrow F\) be the mapping defined by

\[ H(x, y)=\left[\phi_{\beta}(x)\right]^{t} A\left[\phi_{\beta}(y)\right] \quad \text { for all } x, y \in \mathrm{~V} \]

A slight embellishment of the method of Example 2 can be used to prove that \(H \in \mathcal{B}(\mathrm{~V})\). We show that \(\psi_{\beta}(H)=A\). Let \(v_{i}, v_{j} \in \beta\). Then \(\phi_{\beta}\left(v_{i}\right)=e_{i}\) and \(\phi_{\beta}\left(v_{j}\right)=e_{j}\); hence, for any \(i\) and \(j\),

\[ H\left(v_{i}, v_{j}\right)=\left[\phi_{\beta}\left(v_{i}\right)\right]^{t} A\left[\phi_{\beta}\left(v_{j}\right)\right]=e_{i}^{t} A e_{j}=A_{i j} . \]

We conclude that \(\psi_{\beta}(H)=A\) and \(\psi_{\beta}\) is onto. Corollary 1. For any \(n\)-dimensional vector space \(\vee, \mathcal{B}(\mathrm{V})\) has dimension \(n^{2}\).

Proof. Exercise. The following corollary is casily established by reviewing the proof of Theorem 6.32.

Corollary 2. Let V be an \(n\)-dimensional vector space over \(F\) with ordered basis \(\beta\). If \(H \in \mathcal{B}(\mathrm{~V})\) and \(A \in \mathrm{M}_{n \times n}(F)\), then \(\psi_{\beta}(H)=A\) if and only if \(H(x, y)=\left[\phi_{\beta}(x)\right]^{t} A\left[\phi_{\beta}(y)\right]\) for all \(x, y \in \mathrm{~V}\).

The following result is now an immediate consequence of Corollary 2. Corollary 3. Let \(F\) be a field, \(n\) a positive integer, and \(\beta\) be the standard ordered basis for \(F^{n}\). Then for any \(H \in \mathcal{B}\left(F^{n}\right)\), there exists a unique matrix \(A \in \mathrm{M}_{n \times n}(F)\), namely, \(A=\psi_{\beta}(H)\), such that

\[ H(x, y)=x^{t} A y \quad \text { for all } x, y \in \mathrm{~F}^{n} \]

docs.title: Example 4
docs.text:
Define a function \(H: \mathrm{R}^{2} \times \mathrm{R}^{2} \rightarrow R\) by

\[ H\left(\binom{a_{1}}{a_{2}},\binom{b_{1}}{b_{2}}\right)=\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{ll} a_{1} & b_{1} \\ a_{2} & b_{2} \end{array}\right)=a_{1} b_{2}-a_{2} b_{1} \quad \text { for }\binom{a_{1}}{a_{2}},\binom{b_{1}}{b_{2}} \in \mathrm{R}^{2} . \]

It can be shown that \(H\) is a bilinear form. We find the matrix \(A\) in Corollary 3 such that \(H(x, y)=x^{t} A y\) for all \(x, y \in \mathrm{R}^{2}\).

Since \(A_{i j}=H\left(e_{i}, e_{j}\right)\) for all \(i\) and \(j\), we have

\[ \begin{array}{ll} A_{11}=\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right)=0 & A_{12}=\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right)=1, \\ A_{21}=\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right)=-1 & \text { and } \end{array} A_{22}=\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{array}\right)=0 . \]

Thercfore \(A=\left(\begin{array}{rr}0 & 1 \\ -1 & 0\end{array}\right)\). There is an analogy between bilinear forms and linear operators on finitedimensional vector spaces in that both are associated with unique square matrices and the correspondences depend on the choice of an ordered basis for the vector space. As in the case of linear operators, one can pose the following question: How does the matrix corresponding to a fixed bilincar form change when the ordered basis is changed? As we have seen, the corresponding question for matrix representations of linear operators leads to the definition of the similarity relation on square matrices. In the case of bilinear forms, the corresponding question leads to another relation on square matrices, the congruence relation.

Definition. Let \(A, B \in \mathrm{M}_{n \times n}(F)\). Then \(B\) is said to be congruent to \(A\) if there exists an invertible matrix \(Q \in \mathrm{M}_{n \times n}(F)\) such that \(B=Q^{t} A Q\).

Observe that the relation of congruence is an equivalence relation (sec Exercise 12).

The next theorem relates congruence to the matrix representation of a bilinear form.

Theorem 6.33. Let V be a finite-dimensional vector space with ordered bases \(\beta=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}\) and \(\gamma=\left\{w_{1}, w_{2}, \ldots, w_{n}\right\}\), and let \(Q\) be the change of coordinate matrix changing \(\gamma\)-coordinates into \(\beta\)-coordinates. Then, for any \(H \in \mathcal{B}(\mathrm{~V})\), we have \(\dot{\psi}_{\gamma}(H)=Q^{t} \dot{\psi}_{\beta}(H) Q\). Therefore \(\dot{\psi}_{\gamma}(H)\) is congruent to \(\psi_{B}(H)\).

Proof. There are essentially two proofs of this theorem. One involves a direct computation, while the other follows immediately from a clever observation. We give the more direct proof here, leaving the other proof for the excrcises (see Exercise 13).

Suppose that \(A=\psi_{\beta}(H)\) and \(B=\psi_{\gamma}(H)\). Then for \(1 \leq i, j \leq n\),

\[ w_{i}=\sum_{k=1}^{n} Q_{k i} v_{k} \quad \text { and } \quad w_{j}=\sum_{r=1}^{n} Q_{r j} v_{r} . \]

Thus

\[ \begin{aligned} B_{i j} & =H\left(w_{i}, w_{j}\right)=H\left(\sum_{k=1}^{n} Q_{k i} v_{k}, w_{j}\right) \\ & =\sum_{k=1}^{n} Q_{k i} H\left(v_{k}, w_{j}\right) \\ & =\sum_{k=1}^{n} Q_{k i} H\left(v_{k}, \sum_{r=1}^{n} Q_{r j} v_{r}\right) \\ & =\sum_{k=1}^{n} Q_{k i} \sum_{r=1}^{n} Q_{r j} H\left(v_{k}, v_{r}\right) \\ & =\sum_{k=1}^{n} Q_{k i} \sum_{r=1}^{n} Q_{r j} A_{k r} \\ & =\sum_{k=1}^{n} Q_{k i} \sum_{r=1}^{n} A_{k r} Q_{r j} \\ & =\sum_{k=1}^{n} Q_{k i}(A Q)_{k j} \\ & =\sum_{k=1}^{n} Q_{i k}^{t}(A Q)_{k j}=\left(Q^{t} A Q\right)_{i j} . \end{aligned} \]

Hence \(B=Q^{t} A Q\). The following result is the converse of Theorem 6.33. Corollary. Let \(\vee\) be an \(n\)-dimensional vector space with ordered basis \(\beta\), and let \(H\) be a bilinear form on V . For any \(n \times n\) matrix \(B\), if \(B\) is congruent to \(\psi_{\beta}(H)\), then there exists an ordered basis \(\gamma\) for \(\vee\) such that \(\psi_{\gamma}(H)=B\). Furthermore, if \(B=Q^{t} \psi_{\beta}(H) Q\) for some invertible matrix \(Q\), then \(Q\) changes \(\gamma\)-coordinates into \(\beta\)-coordinates.

Proof. Suppose that \(B=Q^{t} \psi_{\beta}(H) Q\) for some invertible matrix \(Q\) and that \(\beta=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}\). Let \(\gamma=\left\{w_{1}, w_{2}, \ldots, w_{n}\right\}\), where

\[ w_{j}=\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_{i j} v_{i} \quad \text { for } 1 \leq j \leq n \]

Since \(Q\) is invertible, \(\gamma\) is an ordered basis for V , and \(Q\) is the change of coordinate matrix that changes \(\gamma\)-coordinates into \(\beta\)-coordinates. Therefore, by Theorem 6.32,

\[ B=Q^{t} \psi_{\beta}(H) Q=\psi_{\gamma}(H) \]

docs.title: Symmetric Bilinear Forms
docs.text:
Like the diagonalization problem for linear operators, there is an analogous diagonalization problem for bilinear forms, namely, the problem of determining those bilinear forms for which there are diagonal matrix representations. As we will sce, there is a close relationship between diagonalizable bilinear forms and those that are called symmetric.

Definition. A bilinear form \(H\) on a vector space V is symmetric if \(H(x, y)=H(y, x)\) for all \(x, y \in \mathrm{~V}\).

As the name suggests, symmetric bilinear forms correspond to symmetric matrices.

Theorem 6.34. Let \(H\) be a bilinear form on a finite-dimensional vector space V , and let \(\beta\) be an ordered basis for V . Then \(H\) is symmetric if and only if \(\psi_{\beta}(H)\) is symmetric.

Proof. Let \(\beta=\left\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \ldots, v_{n}\right\}\) and \(B=\psi^{\prime},_{3}(H)\). First assume that \(H\) is symmetric. Then for \(1 \leq i, j \leq n\),

\[ B_{i j}=H\left(v_{i}, l_{j}\right)=H\left(v_{j}, v_{i}\right)=B_{j i} \]

and it follows that \(B\) is symmetric. Conversely, suppose that \(B\) is symmetric. Let \(J: V \times \mathrm{V} \rightarrow F\), where \(F\) is the field of scalars for V , be the mapping defined by \(J(x, y)=H(y, x)\) for all \(x, y \in \mathrm{~V}\). By property 4 on page \(423, J\) is a bilinear form. Let \(C=\dot{\psi}_{\beta}(J)\). Then, for \(1 \leq i, j \leq n\),

\[ C_{i j}=J\left(v_{i}, v_{j}\right)=H\left(v_{j}, v_{i}\right)=B_{j i}=B_{i j} \]

Thus \(C=B\). Since \(\psi_{\beta}\) is one-to-one, we have \(J=H\). Hence \(H(y, x)=\) \(J(x, y)=H(x, y)\) for all \(x, y \in \mathrm{~V}\), and therefore \(H\) is symmetric.

Definition. A bilinear form \(H\) on a finite-dimensional vector space \(V\) is called diagonalizable if there is an ordered basis \(\beta\) for V such that \(\psi_{\beta}(H)\) is a diagonal matrix.

Corollary. Let \(H\) be a diagonalizable bilinear form on a finite-dimensional vector space V . Then \(H\) is symmetric.

Proof. Suppose that \(H\) is diagonalizable. Then there is an ordered basis \(\beta\) for \(\vee\) such that \(\psi_{\beta}(H)=D\) is a diagonal matrix. Trivially, \(D\) is a symmetric matrix, and hence, by Theorem \(6.34, H\) is symmetric.

Unfortunately, the converse is not true, as is illustrated by the following example.

docs.title: Example 5
docs.text:
Let \(F=Z_{2}, \mathrm{~V}=\mathrm{F}^{2}\), and \(H: \vee imes \vee ightarrow F\) be the bilincar form defined by

\[ H\left( inom{a_{1}}{a_{2}}, inom{b_{1}}{b_{2}} ight)=a_{1} b_{2}+a_{2} b_{1} \]

Clearly \(H\) is symmetric. In fact, if \( eta\) is the standard ordered basis for \(V\), then

\[ A=\psi_{ eta}(H)=\left( egin{array}{ll} 0 & 1 \ 1 & 0 \end{array} ight) \]

a symmetric matrix. We show that \(H\) is not diagonalizable. By way of contradiction, suppose that \(H\) is diagonalizable. Then there is an ordered basis \(\gamma\) for V such that \(B=\psi_{\gamma}(H)\) is a diagonal matrix. So by Theorem 6.33, there exists an invertible matrix \(Q\) such that \(B=Q^{t} A Q\). Since \(Q\) is invertible, it follows that \(\operatorname{rank}(B)=\operatorname{rank}(A)=2\), and consequently the diagonal entries of \(B\) are nonzero. Since the only nonzero scalar of \(F\) is 1 ,

\[ B=\left( egin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \ 0 & 1 \end{array} ight) \]

Suppose that
